description: A health condition 'morashi' when it returns or worsens after improvement within the same understood course. The word connects the change to that course without blaming the person or erasing the improvement that came before it. A new condition or an ordinary setback needs its own account rather than being folded into relapse.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for voiced 'm', carry the sound through the nose, and open into true-mid rounded back 'o'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and open into stressed central 'a'. Raise the tongue blade towards the postalveolar region for unvoiced 'sh', pass air through its central groove, and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
sound_symbolism: The word opens in a rounded hum, broadens at its stressed centre, then narrows into final 'shi'. That turn back towards a finer channel can recall a course reversing after improvement.
usage_notes: Intransitive 'morashi' also supplies the event noun relapse. The condition and earlier improvement identify the course; timing and evidence locate its return. 'nurawi' (chronic) describes persistence or recurrence across a duration rather than this particular reversal.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A changing course can turn without making earlier effort or improvement unreal.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Removing blame from relapse supports honest reporting and continued care instead of shame.

semantic_domains.physical: return or worsening of a health condition after improvement
semantic_domains.temporal: a reversal within an ongoing health course
